response: solution The primary error in the erroneous code is likely the failure to handle cases where the most frequent element appears more than half the time, causing adjacent duplicates when a simple sort-and-interleave approach is used without proper virtual indexing or median selection. solution To correct this, one must first find the median of the array using a linear-time selection algorithm like Quickselect, then reorder the array by placing elements less than the median in odd indices and elements greater than or equal to the median in even indices, while using a virtual index mapping to prevent adjacent duplicates. solution This virtual index mapping transforms the physical index `i` to a logical index `(1 + 2*i) % n` if `n` is odd, or `(1 + 2*i) % (n - 1)` if `n` is even, ensuring that elements with the same value are spaced out. solution For the follow-up requirement of O(n) time without full sorting, the Quickselect algorithm is essential to find the median in linear time, after which the single pass reordering maintains the overall linear complexity. solution This approach also works seamlessly for both positive and negative integers since the comparison logic relies only on relative ordering and not on the sign or magnitude of the values. solution The final implementation guarantees that adjacent elements are unequal whenever a valid wiggle sort exists, satisfying all constraints and edge cases described in the problem.
